OBSERVATORY DISPATCH
Convened in Liberty City
Observatory Dispatch · MMXXVI
"The record was always passing through us."
E5 Enclave
The World's Largest Underground Neutrino Detector Kaiping · Guangdong · 700 m

JUNO

First light from the
ghost-particle deep.

Seven hundred metres beneath southern China, a 35-metre acrylic sphere has returned the most precise measurements yet of how neutrinos change their nature in flight — drawn from just two months of data.

SourceAP News · Nature · Wikipedia
Published10 June 2026
Collaboration750 · 17 nations
CostUS$376M
RecordOpen · Archival

Editor's Preface · E5 Enclave

Render unto the record that which is the record's.

The neutrino is the least of particles. It carries no charge, almost no mass, and passes through the Earth — and through us — without a word. Yet it keeps the oldest account we possess: the first second of the universe, the interior of stars, the heat at the center of the world. The Enclave reads it the way it reads everything. Patiently. Against the calendar of the lineage. What passes through unnoticed is not what fails to matter.

The unseen keeps the record · The center holds

Executive Summary

A detector built to hear the universe's quietest particle has proven it can listen.

On 10 June 2026, the Jiangmen Underground Neutrino Observatory published its first major findings in Nature. The results — drawn from just two months of data collection — represent some of the most precise measurements ever obtained of how neutrinos switch between their three varieties, or "flavors," as they travel through space.

The findings do not yet resolve the longstanding mystery of the neutrino mass hierarchy. But they demonstrate that JUNO's detector is performing at or beyond design specification — and that it will be able to probe the "finer ripples" that separate neutrino flavors and their masses. The headline result is a 1.6-fold improvement in precision on two key oscillation parameters, a sharper measurement than any prior experiment has achieved.

What follows is an open-source briefing on the instrument, the physics, and the decade of discovery it now sets in motion.

Key Findings at a Glance
1.6×
Precision gain on two key
oscillation parameters
2 mo
Of data behind the
first published results
3%
Energy resolution at 1 MeV —
unprecedented for its type
106
Cosmic-muon suppression
from 700 m of rock

01

The Particle

What is a neutrino?

Neutrinos are among the most abundant particles in the universe — and among the most elusive. They are elementary particles that carry no electric charge and have a mass so tiny it was long believed to be zero. They interact with matter only through the weak nuclear force and gravity, which makes them extraordinarily difficult to catch.

A neutrino can pass through a light-year of lead without striking a single atom.The ghost particle

Born in the Big Bang, neutrinos pour out of the Sun, supernovae, reactors, and cosmic-ray collisions. The vast majority pass clean through the planet — and through our bodies — without a single interaction. That ghost-like indifference is why they are so hard to study, and why the puzzle they pose has stood for decades.

100tn
Neutrinos pass through every square centimetre of Earth's surface — every second. Almost none of them notice we are here.

The puzzle deepened in the late 1990s, when experiments proved that neutrinos oscillate — they switch between three "flavors" (electron, muon, and tau) as they travel. That quantum behaviour implies neutrinos have mass, which the original Standard Model never predicted. Pinning down the oscillation, and the masses behind it, is one of the great open questions in physics.

02

The Instrument

A glass sphere, sunk in rock.

JUNO is the world's largest transparent spherical neutrino detector. It sits 52.5 km from both the Yangjiang and Taishan nuclear plants — the optimal distance to read the subtle oscillation of the antineutrinos their reactors emit.

Main sphere diameter35.4 m
Liquid scintillator20,000 t
Photomultiplier tubes~43,200
Underground depth700 m
Distance to reactors52.5 km
Energy resolution3% @ 1 MeV
Construction startJan 2015
Data collection began26 Aug 2025

The 700 m depth and 270 m of granite cut the cosmic-muon background by roughly a million-fold — the ultra-quiet dark JUNO needs to hear a neutrino at all.

03

The Result

What JUNO measured.

JUNO watches electron antineutrinos from the reactors transform into muon and tau flavors as they travel — reading a full spectrum of oscillation from a single fixed baseline, something no prior reactor experiment could do at this precision.

The 2026 results sharpened two key oscillation parameters by a factor of 1.6 over the previous best. More than a number, it is a proof of capability: the detector can resolve the fine, energy-dependent structure of the oscillation pattern — and that structure carries the encoded information about neutrino masses.

The study was led by collaboration scientists including co-author Liangjian Wen, and reviewed by independent physicists including Duke University's Kate Scholberg.

"It really makes me look forward to more exciting results in the future."— Kate Scholberg, Duke University

The mass-hierarchy question

Which ordering is nature's? — not yet resolved

Two masses sit close together; a third stands apart. Is it the heaviest, or the lightest? The 2026 data did not decide — but proved JUNO has the precision to. With years more data, the collaboration expects a 3-sigma or higher determination.

04

The Stakes

Why a ripple in flavor reaches the whole cosmos.

i.

Fundamental Physics

The mass ordering would complete a missing piece of the Standard Model — and could point toward physics beyond it, touching the very stability of matter.

ii.

Cosmology

Second only to photons in abundance, neutrino masses shape how galaxies cluster and how the universe expands — refining models from the Big Bang onward.

iii.

Supernovae

When a star collapses, 99% of its energy leaves as neutrinos. Reading their oscillation is essential to interpreting the next galactic supernova in real time.

iv.

Geoneutrinos

JUNO can detect antineutrinos from radioactive decay inside the Earth — a direct window onto the planet's interior heat budget and deep composition.

v.

Proton Decay

Its great mass and low noise make JUNO sensitive to proton decay — a hypothetical process whose observation would be among the most significant in physics.

✦

The Through-line

One quiet measurement, taken in the dark, reaches from the inside of the Earth to the first second of the universe. That is the reach of getting the small numbers right.

05

Global Context

The neutrino detector race.

JUNO is not alone. Two other giants are rising to cross-check its results — each reading neutrinos through a different medium, at a different scale.

JUNOChina · Live
20,000 t
Liquid scintillator

Reactor antineutrinos at a 52.5 km medium baseline. Operational since August 2025 — the first to report.

Hyper-KJapan · 2027
260,000 t
Water Cherenkov

Successor to the Nobel-winning Super-Kamiokande. Independent verification in water — oscillations, supernovae, proton decay.

DUNEUSA · 2030s
70,000 t
Liquid argon

A 1,300 km beam from Fermilab to South Dakota. The long baseline makes it uniquely sensitive to the mass hierarchy.

Lineage

JUNO follows Daya Bay (China, 2011), which made the first measurement of the oscillation parameter θ₁₃, and RENO (South Korea, 2012), which confirmed it. Originally slated for 2023, JUNO was delayed in construction and finally began collecting data on 26 August 2025.

06

How It Works

The signature of a ghost.

JUNO catches a neutrino through inverse beta decay: an electron antineutrino strikes a proton, producing a positron and a neutron. The two arrive as a tell-tale pair — a prompt flash, then a delayed one — a coincidence that cannot be faked by background.

The detector's 3% energy resolution at 1 MeV is unprecedented for its type — and it is what lets JUNO see the fine structure of the oscillation, the "finer ripples" that encode the mass hierarchy. The challenge is calibration: the tubes must be tuned to better than 1% accuracy across the entire 20,000-tonne volume, achieved with radioactive sources deployed throughout the sphere.

The 52.5 km baseline is no accident. At that distance the oscillation probability for electron antineutrinos reaches its first minimum near 3 MeV — exactly where reactor antineutrinos are most abundant. This "medium baseline" gives JUNO sensitivity to the mass hierarchy without the 1,300 km beam that DUNE requires.

07

Outlook

The golden age, on schedule.

'26–'28

Years 1–3 · Statistics

Accumulate data toward 3-sigma sensitivity on the mass hierarchy. Refine oscillation parameters to sub-percent precision. First measurements of geoneutrino flux from inside the Earth.

'28–'30

Years 3–5 · Determination

Definitive determination of the neutrino mass hierarchy at 5-sigma confidence. Precision measurement of all PMNS matrix elements. Possible signals of neutrinoless double beta decay.

'30–'35

Years 5–10 · Cross-validation

Combined global analysis alongside Hyper-Kamiokande and DUNE. Potential discovery of CP violation in the neutrino sector — which could explain the matter-antimatter asymmetry of the universe.

With JUNO operational, the next decade promises to reshape our picture of the universe's smallest building blocks.
